content.text: that let you ship a new version of an AI agent or LLM-backed service to production while limiting the blast radius of any regression. Rather than replacing all running instances at once, both strategies route only a subset of real traffic to the new version and keep a fast rollback path available at all times.

, two identical environments — blue (current) and green (new) — run in parallel. The load balancer points 100% of traffic at blue. When the green environment has been validated, you flip the load balancer to green in one atomic operation. Blue stays warm for instant rollback.

, you deploy the new version alongside the existing one and shift a small slice of live traffic to it — often 1–5%. You observe real metrics (error rate, latency, LLM eval scores) and gradually increase the canary's traffic share if everything looks healthy, or roll it back to 0% if something breaks.

AI agents are harder to test exhaustively than classical software. A prompt change that passes all offline evals may still degrade quality on the long tail of real user inputs. A new model version may be cheaper on average but catastrophically expensive for a specific request pattern. Without progressive delivery, every deploy is an all-or-nothing bet on your test coverage being complete.
Canary and blue-green deployments create a

between shipping and observing. You discover regressions on a small user population, not your entire base. For agentic systems specifically — where a single workflow may involve multiple LLM calls, tool invocations, and hours of runtime — the cost of a bad deploy is high enough to justify the operational overhead of progressive delivery.
a bad canary at 5% traffic means at most 5% of users are affected before you catch the regression.
both strategies keep a stable version serving traffic throughout the transition. No maintenance window required.
blue-green rollback is a single load-balancer flip (seconds). Canary rollback is setting the canary weight to 0%.
canaries observe actual user inputs, including edge cases and distribution shifts that synthetic tests miss.
you can automate the decision to promote or roll back based on LLM eval scores computed against live traffic samples.
developers and ML engineers can ship prompt/model changes more frequently because the risk of each individual deploy is lower.
both environments must be fully provisioned and ready to serve traffic. For GPU-backed agents, this cost can be significant.
if v2 requires a schema change that is incompatible with v1, the two-environment model breaks. You need backward-compatible migrations first.
weighted traffic splitting requires your load balancer or service mesh to support it. Sticky sessions (where the same user always hits the same version) add further complexity.
a user mid-conversation on v1 cannot be safely migrated to v2 mid-session. You either drain old sessions or accept version mixing within a conversation.
canary deployments are only meaningful if you can measure per-version metrics. Without version-tagged traces, latency histograms, and LLM eval scores, you cannot safely decide when to promote.
a 1% canary on 100 requests per minute gives you only 1 canary request per minute. Statistical significance takes time. Low-traffic services must set a higher initial canary percentage.
Both strategies share the same core mechanism: a

(load balancer, ingress controller, or service mesh) that can send different fractions of requests to different backend pools, with traffic weights configurable at runtime without redeploying the router itself.

: the transition is discrete — 100% blue, then 100% green. The "flip" changes a Kubernetes Service selector, a DNS record, or a load balancer target group binding. It takes effect within seconds and the old environment remains warm until confirmed stable.
: the transition is continuous. A deployment pipeline or controller adjusts the canary weight on a schedule or in response to metric thresholds. Each weight increase is a
(error rate below threshold, eval score above baseline).

attrs.alt: Side-by-side diagram of blue-green (instant LB flip) and canary (weighted traffic split with observability) deployment strategies

content.text: — for example, when deploying an agent that carries session state and a partial migration would corrupt that state.
(different tool schema, different memory format) that cannot coexist with the old version.
but your infrastructure does not support weighted routing.
Your SLA requires rollback in under 30 seconds and you cannot tolerate the lag of scaling down a canary gracefully.
prompt change, model upgrade, or system instruction edit
and want real-traffic quality validation before full rollout.
to get meaningful signal from even a 1–5% slice within your monitoring window.
Your LLM eval pipeline can run
on sampled canary outputs and feed results back to the promotion gate.
— v1 and v2 can share the same database schema and message queues.
Instrument your agent with version tags.
Every trace, log, and metric must carry a
label so you can compare stable vs canary (or blue vs green) in dashboards.
Decide before deployment which metrics must hold for each promotion step: error rate < 2%, LLM eval score >= 0.85, p99 latency <= 4 s.
Deploy the new version alongside the stable version.
For Kubernetes, this means a separate Deployment with its own image tag and version label. For blue-green, name the deployments
Set initial canary weight to 5% (or 0% for blue-green). Use your ingress controller, service mesh, or load balancer's native weight API.

Allow enough real traffic to accumulate statistical confidence. For a service handling 1 000 RPM at 5%, that's 50 canary requests per minute — typically 15–30 minutes for a reliable error rate estimate.
Evaluate and promote or roll back.
Check all metrics against promotion criteria. If they pass, increase the canary weight (e.g. 5% → 25%). If they fail, set weight to 0% and scale the canary deployment to zero.
On full promotion, scale down and delete the stable deployment. On rollback, delete the canary deployment.

# blue-green-deploy.yaml
# Deploy both agent versions as separate Deployments.
# Switch traffic by patching the Service selector.

---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: agent-blue
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: research-agent
      version: blue
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: research-agent
        version: blue
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: agent
          image: myregistry/research-agent:v1.4.2
          env:
            - name: DEPLOYMENT_VERSION
              value: "blue-v1.4.2"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: agent-green
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: research-agent
      version: green
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: research-agent
        version: green
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: agent
          image: myregistry/research-agent:v1.5.0
          env:
            - name: DEPLOYMENT_VERSION
              value: "green-v1.5.0"
---
# Service initially points at blue
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: research-agent
spec:
  selector:
    app: research-agent
    version: blue   # <-- change to "green" to flip
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080
To cut over to green, patch the Service selector:

# Atomic cutover: patch the Service selector to green
kubectl patch service research-agent \
  -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"research-agent","version":"green"}}}'

# Rollback: flip back to blue in seconds
kubectl patch service research-agent \
  -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"research-agent","version":"blue"}}}'
Canary deployment with eval-gated promotion in Python

"""Canary promotion controller.

Polls Prometheus for per-version metrics and promotes or rolls back
the canary Kubernetes deployment based on configurable thresholds.
"""
import time
import subprocess
import requests

PROM_URL = "http://prometheus.monitoring.svc:9090"
NAMESPACE = "prod"
CANARY_DEPLOY = "agent-canary"
STABLE_DEPLOY = "agent-stable"

PROMOTION_STEPS = [5, 25, 50, 100]  # traffic % at each step
SOAK_SECONDS = 900                   # 15 min soak per step
MAX_ERROR_RATE = 0.02                # 2% error threshold
MIN_EVAL_SCORE = 0.85                # LLM eval pass-rate threshold


def query_prometheus(promql: str) -> float:
    resp = requests.get(f"{PROM_URL}/api/v1/query", params={"query": promql})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    results = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
    if not results:
        return 0.0
    return float(results[0]["value"][1])


def get_canary_metrics() -> dict:
    error_rate = query_prometheus(
        'rate(http_requests_total{version="canary",status=~"5.."}[5m])'
        ' / rate(http_requests_total{version="canary"}[5m])'
    )
    eval_score = query_prometheus(
        'avg(llm_eval_score{version="canary"}[5m])'
    )
    p99_latency = query_prometheus(
        'histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{version="canary"}[5m]))'
    )
    return {"error_rate": error_rate, "eval_score": eval_score, "p99_latency": p99_latency}


def set_canary_weight(weight_pct: int) -> None:
    """Update Nginx Ingress canary weight annotation."""
    subprocess.run([
        "kubectl", "annotate", "ingress", "research-agent",
        f"nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight={weight_pct}",
        "--overwrite", f"-n={NAMESPACE}",
    ], check=True)
    print(f"  Canary weight set to {weight_pct}%")


def promote_canary() -> None:
    for step in PROMOTION_STEPS:
        set_canary_weight(step)
        print(f"Soaking at {step}% for {SOAK_SECONDS}s...")
        time.sleep(SOAK_SECONDS)

        metrics = get_canary_metrics()
        print(f"  Metrics: {metrics}")

        if metrics["error_rate"] > MAX_ERROR_RATE:
            print(f"ERROR RATE {metrics['error_rate']:.2%} > {MAX_ERROR_RATE:.2%} — rolling back")
            set_canary_weight(0)
            return

        if metrics["eval_score"] < MIN_EVAL_SCORE:
            print(f"EVAL SCORE {metrics['eval_score']:.2f} < {MIN_EVAL_SCORE} — rolling back")
            set_canary_weight(0)
            return

        print(f"  Step {step}% PASSED")

    print("Canary promoted to 100% — scaling down stable deployment")
    subprocess.run(
        ["kubectl", "scale", f"deployment/{STABLE_DEPLOY}", "--replicas=0", f"-n={NAMESPACE}"],
        check=True,
    )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    promote_canary()
Feature-flag canary for LLM prompt changes

"""Route a percentage of requests to a new prompt without redeploying.

Uses a deterministic hash of the user ID so the same user always
experiences the same version within a session.
"""
import hashlib
import os
import anthropic

CANARY_PCT = int(os.environ.get("CANARY_PCT", "5"))  # default 5%

PROMPT_V1 = """You are a research assistant. Answer the question concisely."""
PROMPT_V2 = """You are an expert research assistant. Answer with step-by-step reasoning,
citing specific evidence where possible."""


def is_canary(user_id: str) -> bool:
    """Deterministic bucket assignment: user always lands in same cohort."""
    bucket = int(hashlib.md5(user_id.encode()).hexdigest(), 16) % 100
    return bucket < CANARY_PCT


def run_agent(user_id: str, question: str) -> dict:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    in_canary = is_canary(user_id)
    system_prompt = PROMPT_V2 if in_canary else PROMPT_V1
    version = "canary-v2" if in_canary else "stable-v1"

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    answer = response.content[0].text

    # Emit a version-tagged metric for your observability stack
    print(f"[{version}] user={user_id} input_tokens={response.usage.input_tokens}")

    return {"answer": answer, "version": version}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # 5% of requests will use the new prompt
    result = run_agent(user_id="user-42", question="What is RAG?")
    print(result["version"], result["answer"][:80])

a production research agent uses Claude claude-sonnet-4-5 with a system prompt that instructs it to return structured JSON. The team wants to migrate to a new system prompt that also includes chain-of-thought reasoning before the JSON block. Offline evals on 500 golden examples show a 4% improvement in answer quality, but they want real-traffic confirmation before migrating all users.
They run a canary at 5% for 30 minutes (stable at 95%), collecting:
Token cost per request (canary prompt is longer; expected 15% more tokens)
JSON parse success rate (new prompt must still emit valid JSON)
Online LLM judge score (a secondary Claude call rates answer quality on a 1-5 scale)
p99 latency (longer prompt = longer TTFB; must stay under 6 s)

"""Online eval judge that scores canary outputs and feeds back to the
promotion controller via a Prometheus push gateway."""
import anthropic
import requests
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
PUSHGW = "http://pushgateway.monitoring.svc:9091"


def judge_output(question: str, answer: str, version: str) -> float:
    """Ask Claude to rate the answer quality on a 0-1 scale."""
    judge_prompt = f"""Rate the following research answer on a scale of 0 to 1.
0 = wrong or unhelpful. 1 = accurate, complete, well-reasoned.
Respond with ONLY a JSON object: {{"score": <float>}}

Question: {question}
Answer: {answer}"""

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=64,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": judge_prompt}],
    )
    score = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)["score"]

    # Push to Prometheus push gateway with version label
    payload = (
        f'# TYPE llm_eval_score gauge\n'
        f'llm_eval_score{{version="{version}"}} {score}\n'
    )
    requests.post(
        f"{PUSHGW}/metrics/job/canary_eval/instance/{version}",
        data=payload,
    )
    return score


# Usage inside the agent request handler:
# score = judge_output(question, answer, version="canary-v2")
# promotion controller reads avg(llm_eval_score{version="canary-v2"}[5m]) from Prometheus

After 30 minutes the canary shows: JSON parse success 99.8% (pass), judge score 4.3/5 vs 4.0/5 stable (pass), token cost +17% (acceptable), p99 latency 5.2 s (within threshold). The controller promotes to 25%, waits another 15 minutes, promotes to 50%, then 100%. Total wall-clock time from first deploy to full rollout: 90 minutes with zero manual intervention after the initial canary weight was set.
tag on every metric, you cannot distinguish canary degradation from stable degradation, and the promotion gate becomes meaningless.
a 60-second soak at 5% traffic on a 100 RPM service gives only 5 canary samples — not enough to estimate error rate reliably. Use at least 10 minutes per step, 30 minutes for low-traffic services.
Using the same Kubernetes Service for blue and green:
if both Deployments share a Service selector label, the Service load-balances across both. Blue-green requires two
label values, not a shared one.
deleting the stable deployment while live sessions are in-flight on it drops those connections. Set
long enough for in-progress agent runs (often 5–10 minutes for long-running workflows).
a new model or prompt can be error-free but still produce lower-quality answers. Always include a quality metric (LLM eval score, task success rate) alongside technical metrics.
a new prompt that uses 3x more tokens passes all quality thresholds but triples your LLM bill. Include token count or dollar cost per request in your promotion criteria.
Thresholds set after you see the canary data are subject to confirmation bias. Agree on pass criteria in the PR that ships the change.
Keep canary and stable on the same hardware class.
Latency comparisons are only valid if both versions run on identical instance types.
A human watching dashboards at 2am will be too slow. Wire the promotion controller to call the rollback path automatically when any threshold is breached.
Use sticky sessions for conversational agents.
Route a user to the same version for the duration of their session. Hash the session ID instead of the request ID for the bucket assignment.
Apply backward-compatible schema changes (additive columns, nullable fields) before deploying v2. Never run a migration that breaks v1 while v1 is still serving traffic.
Log which version handled each request.
Include the version tag in the response headers or audit log so you can reproduce canary outputs during post-incident analysis.
Both strategies add routing overhead that is negligible compared to LLM latency (a few microseconds at the load balancer or ingress). The more significant resource considerations are:
Blue-green doubles resource consumption during the overlap window.
For GPU-backed model servers, this can mean 2x GPU hours for the transition period. Plan to keep the overlap as short as practical — typically 15–60 minutes.
Canary requires at minimum 1 extra replica
to receive its traffic share. If your stable fleet has 10 pods and you canary at 10%, the canary needs at least 1 pod (which will be serving 10% of requests).
Online eval judging adds LLM cost.
If you sample 100% of canary outputs for a judge call, you double the LLM cost for that traffic slice. Subsample (e.g. 20% of canary requests) or use a smaller/faster judge model like Haiku.
Horizontal scaling still applies within each version.
The canary Deployment can have its own HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler). As the canary weight grows, the HPA scales it up to handle the increased share.
Declarative canary and blue-green with analysis runs
Native Prometheus analysis; most complete open-source option
Tight service-mesh integration; supports webhooks for custom analysis
Fine-grained weighted routing with header-based rules
Pairs well with Argo Rollouts or Flagger
Simple setup; no service mesh required
Canary and blue-green for ECS, Lambda, EC2
Built-in alarm-based rollback; integrates with ALB
Best for prompt/model switch; no infra duplication
Secret rotation must apply to both versions simultaneously.
If you rotate an API key or database credential, both the stable and canary deployments need the new secret. Use Kubernetes Secrets with live mounts rather than baking secrets into images.
The canary pod's service account needs the same permissions as stable. Audit access policies before promoting to higher traffic shares.
Rate-limit per version, not just globally.
A runaway canary that burns through your LLM rate limit will starve the stable deployment. Apply separate rate-limit quotas to each version at the gateway.
Health checks must pass before canary receives traffic.

Configure Kubernetes readiness probes so the canary pod only enters the load balancer rotation after it has successfully handled a startup health check. A pod that crashes on boot should never receive user traffic.
Compliance and incident investigation require knowing which version of the agent processed a specific user request. Include

A prompt injection vulnerability in the new version affects at most the canary traffic slice (e.g. 5% of users) before the anomaly detector rolls it back — a meaningful security benefit of progressive delivery.

Blue-green gives you an instant, atomic cutover and fast rollback by flipping a load balancer selector between two fully provisioned environments at the cost of ~2x infrastructure during the transition window.

Canary gives you real-traffic validation by routing a small percentage of live requests to the new version, observing per-version metrics, and promoting or rolling back based on whether those metrics meet predefined thresholds.

For LLM agents, promotion criteria must go beyond error rate and latency to include quality metrics — LLM eval scores, task success rate, token cost — because an agent can be technically healthy but semantically degraded.
Every metric and log entry must carry a version tag; without it, you cannot separate canary signal from stable baseline and the promotion gate has no reliable input.

Automate rollback: a human watching dashboards is too slow when an agent is burning through rate limits or serving bad responses at scale. Wire the promotion controller to set the canary weight to 0 automatically when any threshold is breached.
Feature flags are the lightweight alternative for prompt-only changes — route a percentage of users to a different system prompt in-process, with zero infra overhead and instant flag-flip rollback.
Apply backward-compatible database migrations before the canary deploy, not concurrently — never put v1 and v2 in production simultaneously against a schema that only v2 understands.
